title: Going, going, gone – thousands of jobs slashed as banks look to bottom line
full_text:
Employees of the big four banks survived the worst ever year for job losses after nearly 7800 bank staff lost their jobs in 2025-26, according to the Finance Sector Union.
Real job losses across the sector are thought to be far greater beyond the disclosed redundancies.
Job losses are also expected to continue over this financial year, as banks look for new ways to juice profits amid predictions economic activity is set to idle.
FSU national secretary Julia Angrisano said the job losses were good news for the bottom line. “For bank employees this has been the worst year ever for job losses,” she said.
“The banks will tell you those profits are the result of more customers and cost efficiencies.
“But I think it’s clear that throwing staff on the unemployment scrap heap also contributes in a big way towards the billions of dollars they hoard each year and the multimillion-dollar bonuses the bosses award themselves.” The FSU figures only capture redundancy rounds reported to the union. Among these is Westpac, which kicked off a cost-cutting round expected to affect 1500 staff. The FSU said it had only been notified of 779 affected by Westpac.
A Westpac spokesman did not respond to requests to disclose how many jobs are being lost.
Financial accounts show Westpac’s staff numbers declined by 299 between September 2025 and March 2026.
Rival Commonwealth Bank cut 735 staff last financial year, according to the FSU. The union said this was across CBA, as well as its subsidiaries Bankwest and Colonial First State. CBA shut or converted most of the Bankwest branches to CBA-branded sites last year as it shifted its brand to an online-only model. A CBA spokesman declined to comment.
The bank has also come under fire for plans to shift work to its Indian subsidiaries. CBA reports annual profit on August 12.
Last week, the FSU warned CBA was slashing Australian staff in technology roles while recruiting for similar roles in India.
National Australia Bank had slashed 1775 jobs last financial year while bulking up in Vietnam and India. NAB has 5000 workers in Gurugram and Bengaluru, and 2000 in Ho Chi Minh City and Hanoi. NAB declined to comment.
ANZ’s overhaul represented the majority of the job cuts across the banking sector, after the bank’s new chief executive, Nuno Matos, slashed 3500 staff and 1000 contractors and consultants.
Mr Matos pitched the job cuts as necessary to eliminate complexity, but triggered union anger over ANZ’s failure to consult.
ANZ’s March accounts show the bank had 2170 fewer staff than it did in September 2025.
ANZ appears to have recently resumed reducing headcount, however a spokeswoman declined to comment.
Atlas Funds Management chief investment officer Hugh Dive said all banks were looking at job cuts as a way to boost earnings amid weak economic prospects for Australia.
Mr Dive pointed to the big bank branch networks as vulnerable, albeit closures have slowed.
Some banks, such as CBA, have looked to artificial intelligence as an opportunity to swing the axe.
Mr Dive said this was likely to persist. “If it’s an area where they can benefit through AI and processing, then it’s no issue,” he said.
But he warned that misplaced job cuts could result in greater fraud or loan losses.
The major banks are thought to have been subject to an orchestrated scheme to use AI to fabricate documents in a bid to fraudulently secure loans.
“It’ll be interesting to see whether AI can improve fraud detection,” Mr Dive said. In a recent note to clients, Bank of America analyst Matt Dunger warned Australia’s banks faced a rising number of bad debts and worsening profit margins.
